<!-- =========================================================================
     COMPONENT GALLERY
     Every component below works in BOTH the PDF and the HTML:
       - HTML  : the fenced-div class is styled by styles.css
       - PDF   : inline.lua rewrites the class into a Typst function (style.typ)
     ========================================================================= -->

## Inline text: code, badges, subtext & labels

Inline `code spans` get a rounded, theme-aware background (via `inline.lua`, which
tags them `.inline-code` on the web and wraps them in `#InlineCode` in the PDF).
You can also highlight a term as an [orange badge]{.orange-badge}, add quieter
[explanatory subtext]{.subtext}, or use monospace section labels like
[INPUT]{.label-orange}, [CONTEXT]{.label-blue}, and [NOTE]{.label-dark}.

<!-- Syntax: `code` Â· [text]{.orange-badge} Â· [text]{.subtext} Â·
     [text]{.label-orange|.label-blue|.label-dark} -->

## Callout / boxed paragraphs

A **prompt box** is a left-accented panel â€” good for system prompts, definitions,
or any block you want to set apart. Wrap content in `::: {.prompt-box}`.

::: {.prompt-box}
Use the context below to answer the question.

Answer with only the letter of the correct choice (A, B, C, or D).

Context: {context}

Question: {question}
:::

A **research-question / outlined box** (`::: {.research-question-box}`) is a plain
bordered, italic block for a thesis statement or key question.

::: {.research-question-box}
How can a single Markdown source produce both a polished PDF and a themeable
website without maintaining two documents?
:::

An **algorithm box** (`::: {.algorithm-box}`) is a light panel for pseudocode. It
respects manual line breaks (end a line with a backslash `\`), inline and display
math, monospace step labels like `` `1:` ``, `$\quad$` for indentation, and a
horizontal rule (`---`) to split sub-procedures (styled by `.algorithm-box hr`).

::: {.algorithm-box}
**Algorithm 1** FederatedAveraging. $K$ clients indexed by $k$; local batch size
$B$, local epochs $E$, learning rate $\eta$.

**Server executes:**\
`1:` initialize $w_0$\
`2:` **for** each round $t = 1, 2, \dots$ **do**\
`3:` $\quad$ $S_t \leftarrow$ (random set of $m$ clients)\
`4:` $\quad$ **for** each client $k \in S_t$ **in parallel do**\
`5:` $\quad\quad$ $w_{t+1}^{k} \leftarrow \text{ClientUpdate}(k, w_t)$\
`6:` $\quad$ $w_{t+1} \leftarrow \sum_{k} \frac{n_k}{n}\, w_{t+1}^{k}$
:::

```{=typst}
#pagebreak()
```

## Data cards (colored top border)

Cards use `::: {.custom-card}` plus a theme class: `.anchor-theme` (blue),
`.positive-theme` (green), or `.negative-theme` (red). Combine with Quarto's
`layout-ncol` to place them side by side; a `---` inside becomes a divider rule.

::: {.list-indent}
::: {layout-ncol="3"}

::: {.custom-card .anchor-theme}
**Anchor** [(neutral)]{.subtext}

---

The blue "anchor" theme is for a reference item or a query.
:::

::: {.custom-card .positive-theme}
**Positive** [(good)]{.subtext}

---

The green "positive" theme is for a correct or desirable item.
:::

::: {.custom-card .negative-theme}
**Negative** [(bad)]{.subtext}

---

The red "negative" theme is for an incorrect or cautionary item.
:::

:::
:::

## Cards as a numbered figure (Q&A / quiz)

Put cards inside a `::: {#fig-...}` block to get a numbered, cross-referenceable
figure (@fig-quiz). This example pairs a `.quiz-card` with `.mcq-option` answers;
add `.correct` to the right one.

::: {#fig-quiz}

::: {layout="[[45, 55]]"}

::: {.quiz-card}
[Topic Tag]{.quiz-tag} [Subtopic]{.quiz-tag}

[Question]{.label-orange}
Which fenced-div class produces the green "correct answer" styling?

[Context]{.label-blue}
Options use `::: {.mcq-option}`; the correct one adds the `.correct` class.
:::

::: {.quiz-card}

::: {.mcq-option}
**Option A** `.custom-card`
:::
::: {.mcq-option .correct}
**Option B (correct)** `.mcq-option .correct`
:::
::: {.mcq-option}
**Option C** `.prompt-box`
:::

> **Explanation**
> `.mcq-option .correct` renders the green border and background.
:::

:::

A quiz card paired with multiple-choice options.
:::

## Author / bio strip

`.author-box` (a shaded pill) and `.divider-line` (a full-width rule) are HTML-only
utilities â€” handy for a byline or a contributor list. The raw Typst counterpart to the former (other than using `backticks like this`) is a `box` (optionally in a `grid`), which lets you control the fill color, radius, etc.:

::: {.content-visible when-format="typst"}
```{=typst}
#grid(
     columns: 2,
     align: horizon,
     gutter: 1.2em, 
     box(
     fill: rgb(155, 155, 155, 40), 
     inset: (x: 50pt, y: 8pt), 
     radius: 6pt, 
     text(fill: rgb("#474747b8"))[Your Name]
     ),
     text(fill: rgb("#000000b8"))[Your Role / Affiliation]
)
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
```{=html}
<p><span class="author-box"><a href="mailto:you@example.com">Your Name</a></span> â€” Your Role / Affiliation</p>
<div class="divider-line"></div>
```
:::
